title: A quantitative analysis of intensification in the ethnographic record
methods: multiple linear regression with categorical technology factor
energy unit harmonization (MJ equivalents for human, animal, machine labour)
settlement urbanization rate estimation from hierarchical settlement data
population growth regression against Ethnographic Atlas agricultural intensity index
data_sources: Custom ethnographic compilation (520 food production case studies, 40 societies)
Binford hunter-gatherer dataset (territory area / population density)
Sandeford settlement data (early-state urbanization in 6 world regions)
UNL Tractor Test Records (one early-industrial Kansas case, 1920s)

data_scope.temporal_scope: 20th-century ethnographic record; archaeological estimates to ~5,500 BP for early states
data_scope.data_availability: All data in published Supplementary XLSX; R code in Supplementary Software

core_conclusion: Across 520 food production case studies in 40 societies from foraging bands to agricultural states, labour productivity increases with intensification — rejecting the standard model's diminishing-returns assumption. A cultural niche construction model derived from settlement scaling theory (A = aN^alpha, alpha approx 0.5, beta approx 0.75) accurately predicts the power-law relationship between energy input and output, explaining over 95% of variance in food energy output. Intensification is an autocatalytic feedback engine, not a response to scarcity.
why_it_matters: First comprehensive quantitative test of the standard intensification model across the ethnographic record; resolves a decades-long debate by rejecting the standard model and supporting a mathematically grounded alternative; links the Neolithic demographic transition, urban emergence, and division of labour within a single mechanistic framework; provides defensible quantitative benchmarks (4x productivity, 1000x intensity, 4x population per major energy transition) for archaeological inference.
why_nature: Genuinely cross-disciplinary synthesis unifying anthropology, macroecology, behavioural ecology and cultural evolutionary theory; transforms a qualitative debate into a formal statistical test; findings span the full complexity gradient from hunter-gatherers to agricultural states with universal implications; open data and reproducible R code meet journal reproducibility standards.
added_value_to_my_research: Beta approx 0.76 sub-linear scaling of food output to input calibrates demand-surface assumptions in 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility models
CNC power-law production function provides a physics-informed prior for GeoAI models predicting urban service demand in data-scarce settings
Institutional-context variable (epsilon) suggests grouping variable for distance-decay heterogeneity in healthcare accessibility models across diverse cultural settings
Autocatalytic innovation-rate scaling provides macroecological grounding for spatiotemporal GeoAI models of rapidly urbanizing regions (e.g., Global South)
Density-area scaling law (A = aN^alpha) connects archaeological urbanization theory to my spatial equity optimization for facility location under density gradients

related.title: Explaining the prevalence, scaling and variance of urban phenomena
related.relation: Unifies economic complexity and cultural evolution to derive urban scaling laws — directly extends the CNC model's power-law framework to modern urban systems

related.title: Simple spatial scaling rules behind complex cities
related.relation: Provides network-theoretic machinery for testing food production scaling laws in modern urban contexts

related.title: Subsistence styles shape human social learning strategies
related.relation: Shows subsistence technology shapes information-acquisition behavior — complementary to the CNC model's institutional-context (epsilon) dimension

related.title: Parallel scaling of elite wealth in ancient Roman and modern cities with implications for understanding urban inequality
related.relation: Links food surplus and urbanization rates to emergent wealth inequality via urban scaling — extends the paper's productivity benchmarks to social stratification

related.title: Underlying rules of evolutionary urban systems in Africa
related.relation: Documents Zipf's law and Gibrat's law in African urban systems — validation context for CNC model scaling predictions in contemporary Global South urbanization

figures.caption: The standard and cultural niche construction models illustrated — contrasts the diminishing-returns trajectory (standard model) with the continually innovating niche-construction trajectory (CNC model)

Intensity of food production vs. labour productivity across four technological contexts (n=204/215/86/16 for foraging/extensive/intensive/early-industrial) — labour productivity rises with intensification
Mechanisms and mathematics of the CNC model dynamics — the autocatalytic feedback loop linking niche construction, labour productivity, population growth, and political innovation
